shows.t: On a Clear Day You Can See Forever
shows.syn: ;Act I Quirky Daisy Gamble sees herself as an unremarkable person and has low self-esteem, even though she can (1) make plants grow remarkably, (2) predict when a telephone will ring or someone will drop in, and (3) tell where to find an object that someone else is looking for. Her current problem, though, is her nasty smoking habit, which will interfere with the chances of her fiancé, Warren, for a job with great benefits.

The infamous scheming masterminds captured a nation’s heart during the Great Depression. Decades on, Bonnie and Clyde continue to be celebrated in a gritty, tense musical that puts their lives centre stage.

PRIVATE LIVES premiered on August 18, 1930 at the King’s Theatre in Edinburgh. The play debuted on Broadway at the Times Square Theatre on January 27, 1931. Private Lives is about Elyot and Amanda, once married and now honeymooning with new spouses at the same hotel. What happens when they meet in these new circumstances results in a sparkling roundelay that results in what critics and audiences consider Coward’s greatest comedy.

"Venus in Fur" is a tantalizing theatrical experience that delves into the captivating dynamic between a director and an audacious actress. As the two engage in a charged power struggle, the line between fantasy and reality blurs, unleashing a thrilling exploration of desire, dominance, and the complexities of the creative process. Audiences are swept into a captivating world where the pursuit of artistic vision collides with the intoxicating allure of submission, leaving them spellbound and eager to uncover the hidden depths of this gripping two-person drama.

=== Act I === The show opens with the "Opening (Monologue)", as God's voice, spoken by Jesus, declaring his supremacy: "My name is Known: God and King. I am most in majesty, in whom no beginning may be and no end. " The cast then enters and takes the roles of various philosophers who sing their philosophies, first alone, then in cacophonous counterpoint ("Prologue: Tower of Babble").

An evening of three one-act comedies by Ethan Coen, Elaine May, and Woody Allen, directed by John Turturro. The production features "Talking Cure" (Coen), "George is Dead" (May), and "Honeymoon Motel" (Allen).

=== Act I === The Mountaintop begins with Martin Luther King Jr. returning to the Lorraine Motel after his sermon, I've Been To The Mountaintop. King sends his friend to fetch him cigarettes, ultimately leaving him alone in Room 306 as a storm rages outside. Ordering room service, King comes into contact with Camae (Carrie Mae), the mysterious and beautiful maid who strikes King's attention immediately.
Man and Boy is a play by Terence Rattigan. It was first performed at The Queen's Theatre, London, and Brooks Atkinson Theatre, New York, in 1963, with Charles Boyer starring as Gregor Antonescu.

The opera diva Maria Callas, a glamorous, commanding, larger-than-life, caustic, and surprisingly funny pedagogue is holding a singing master class. Alternately dismayed and impressed by the students who parade before her, she retreats into recollections about the glories of her own life and career.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre
shows.syn: The People in the Picture is a musical with book and lyrics by Iris Rainer Dart and music by Mike Stoller and Artie Butler. The musical is about a grandmother recalling her life in the Yiddish theater and the Holocaust.

ct.n: Howell Binkley and James F. Ingalls

shows.syn: This is must-see theatre at its best, as these powerhouse actors lead this searing production about sparring, unlikely lovers. See these performers like you’ve never seen them before in a play The New York Times called “the equivalent of sitting at ringside watching a prize fight.”

Born Yesterday may refer to: * Born Yesterday (play), a 1946 Broadway play by Garson Kanin ** Born Yesterday (1950 film), a film directed by George Cukor ** Born Yesterday (1956 film), a TV film directed by Garson Kanin ** Born Yesterday (1993 film), a film directed by Luis Mandoki * Born Yesterday (album), a 1985 album by The Everly Brothers * Born Yesterday (song), a song by The Everly Brothers * "Born Yesterday", a song by Quadeca from the 2022 album I Didn't Mean to Haunt You * Born Yesterda

High is a 2010 American play written by Matthew Lombardo. The story revolves around a nun, Sister Jamison Connelly, who deals with her sordid past and the people around her with her acerbic wit and wisdom.

Join curious Alice as she encounters talking animals, mad tea parties, and the mysterious Queen of Hearts in a fantastical world brought to life through enchanting songs, colorful characters, and delightful surprises for all ages.

War Horse is a play based on the book of the same name by writer Michael Morpurgo, adapted for stage by Nick Stafford. Originally Morpurgo thought "they must be mad" to try to make a play from his best-selling 1982 novel; but the play was a great success.

In this gritty, biting comedy, a man returns home to find another man's hat in his apartment, igniting a wild goose chase through the streets of New York as he confronts his girlfriend, his sponsor, and his best friend, all while struggling to maintain his newfound sobriety. With razor-sharp dialogue and relatable characters, "The Motherfucker with the Hat" takes a sharp-witted look at the complexities of love, addiction, and the bonds of friendship.
Catch Me If You Can is a musical drama with a libretto by Terrence McNally and a theatrical score by Marc Shaiman and Scott Wittman. It follows the story of a con artist named Frank Abagnale.

Five versions of the libretto of Anything Goes exist: the original 1934 libretto, the 1962 off-Broadway revival libretto, the 1987 revival libretto, the 2011 revival libretto, and the 2022 revised libretto. The story has been revised, though all involve similar romantic complications aboard the SS American and feature the same major characters.

shows.t: Bengal Tiger at the Baghdad Zoo

ct.n: Acme Sound Partners and Cricket S. Myers

shows.t: How to Succeed in Business Without Really Trying

shows.syn: From the creators of South Park comes this hilarious musical about two mismatched Mormon missionaries sent to spread the word in a remote Ugandan village.
That Championship Season is a 1972 play by Jason Miller. It was the recipient of the 1973 Pulitzer Prize for Drama and the 1973 Tony Award for Best Play.

Three-time Olivier Award-winner Sharon D Clarke is joined by Ncuti Gatwa (Doctor Who; Sex Education) in this joyful reimagining of Oscar Wilde’s most celebrated comedy, a hilarious story of identity, impersonation, and romance, filmed live from the National Theatre in London.

Elling is a 2007 theatre adaptation by Simon Bent of the 2001 film of the same name. The film and play is based on the book of the same name of Norwegian author Ingvar Ambjørnsen. A different (and less successful) version of the play premiered on Broadway on November 21, 2010 using a completely different cast and director, it closed on November 28, 2010, after 9 performances and 22 previews.

In the zany world of "The Pee-wee Herman Show," audiences join the iconic bow-tied eccentric on a whimsical adventure filled with quirky characters, offbeat humor, and imaginative set pieces. As Pee-wee navigates the colorful world of his Playhouse, he faces unexpected challenges and encounters a diverse array of friends, foes, and peculiar personalities, all while captivating audiences with his boundless energy and childlike wonder. This beloved stage production promises a delightfully surreal and uproariously entertaining theatrical experience for fans of Pee-wee Herman and newcomers alike.

shows.t: Women on the Verge of a Nervous Breakdown
shows.syn: Pepa and Iván are voice actors who dub foreign films, most recently Johnny Guitar with Joan Crawford and Sterling Hayden. Depressed because Iván has left her, Pepa oversleeps, missing a recording session with Iván, and receiving a voicemail message from him asking her to pack his things in a suitcase he will pick up.

1948 Atlanta, Georgia. A crash is heard, and Daisy Werthan, age 72, is in her living room, with her son Boolie, age 40. She has crashed her car, and Boolie insists that she have a driver. Boolie is in his office and interviews Hoke Coleburn who is a black man of around 60. He is unemployed. Over the next 25 years Hoke drives "Miss Daisy".

In the exuberant world of 17th-century France, a renowned playwright, Elomire, finds his rigid artistic principles challenged by the brash and bombastic Valere, a self-proclaimed genius who infiltrates the court. As the two engage in a battle of wits and egos, their clash of artistic visions threatens to upend the very fabric of the theatrical establishment. With biting wit and uproarious humor, "La Bête" delves into the timeless tensions between tradition and innovation, offering a delightful commentary on the nature of art and the human condition.

ct.n: Michael Friedman, Gabriel Kahane and Justin Levine

shows.syn:
A Life in the Theatre is a 1977 play by David Mamet.
It focuses on the relationship between two actors, the play's only characters. One, Robert, is a stage veteran while John is a young, promising actor.

Five-time Olivier Award winner Imelda Staunton (The Crown) joins forces with her real-life daughter Bessie Carter (Bridgerton) for the very first time, playing mother and daughter in Bernard Shaw’s incendiary moral classic.

"The Pitmen Painters" transports audiences to 1930s Northern England, where a group of coal miners embark on an unexpected artistic journey. Discovering a talent for painting, they challenge societal norms and explore the transformative power of art, navigating the complexities of class, education, and self-expression. Through captivating storytelling and vivid onstage depictions, the play offers a compelling exploration of the human spirit's ability to find expression in the most unexpected of circumstances.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre
shows.syn: In a railway station refreshment room, a man and a woman are sitting glumly at a table. One of the woman's friends comes in and immediately begins chatting away. After some terse pleasantries, the man leaves to catch his train. The woman explains that the man is about to move to Africa. After the man leaves, the woman abruptly disappears but soon returns, explaining that she wanted to see the express train pass by.

ct.n: Ashley Hanson, Kurt Eric Fischer and Brian Ronan
Ashley Hanson , Kurt Eric Fischer and Brian Ronan

shows.syn: The play concerns the financial scandal and collapse of Enron, the American energy corporation, based in Texas. Enron executive Jeffrey Skilling and his boss Kenneth Lay are shown, as well as Skilling's protégé Andy Fastow, who rises to become the chief financial officer. === Act One === A lawyer tells the audience about world-changing.

===Act I=== Chuck Baxter is an ambitious bachelor and junior executive for a large insurance company, Consolidated Life, who expresses his frustrations and hopes for career advancement ("Half as Big as Life"). To curry favor with higher-ups in the company, he allows his apartment to be used for their romantic trysts in return for promises of promotion ("Upstairs").

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre

shows.syn: La Cage aux Folles may refer to: * La Cage aux Folles (play), 1973 French play * La Cage aux Folles (film), 1978 French-Italian film adapted from the play **La Cage aux Folles II, 1980 sequel **La Cage aux Folles 3: 'Elles' se marient, 1985 sequel * La Cage aux Folles (musical), 1983 musical adapted from the play

On December 4, 1956, Johnny Cash, Jerry Lee Lewis, Carl Perkins and Elvis Presley assemble at the Sun Record recording studio in Memphis, Tennessee. Established performer Carl Perkins was there to record songs with a new performer, Jerry Lee Lewis. Elvis Presley stops by the studio along with his girlfriend, a singer named Dyanne (at the real session, Elvis’ girlfriend was a dancer, Marilyn Evans—now Riehl).

=== Broadway version === Setting: The Addams Family Mansion, Central Park, New York City ====Act I==== The ghoulish Addams family are visiting the graveyard for an annual gathering of all family members (living, dead, and undecided) to celebrate what it is to be an Addams ("When You're an Addams").

"Lend Me a Tenor" is a hilarious farce that takes place in a Cleveland hotel suite. When a famous Italian opera singer is unable to perform, a young assistant is tasked with impersonating him, leading to a night of mistaken identities, romantic hijinks, and side-splitting laughter. With a talented cast and a delightfully absurd plot, this Broadway production promises an evening of pure entertainment.

The sitcom is based around a modern family who live in the Midlands. The family consists of Colin Craddock, a white Brummie builder, and his Asian wife Rupinder. Colin's two sons from his first marriage, Peter and Leo, share the house, as do Rupinder's half-sister Sima, daughter Kavita and son Raj, a wheelchair user with cerebral palsy.
Next Fall is a play written by Geoffrey Nauffts. The play is about two gay men in a committed relationship with a twist, with one, Luke, being devoutly religious and the other, Adam, an atheist.

A mysterious stranger seeks a hand that was severed from his body decades ago. As he confronts a couple of shady con artists who claim to have the missing hand, a twisted game of deceit and violence unfolds, leading to a shocking and darkly comedic climax. With its offbeat characters and unsettling premise, "A Behanding in Spokane" promises a gripping theatrical experience that will leave audiences on the edge of their seats.

"The Miracle Worker" follows the powerful true story of Helen Keller, a young girl who is deaf and blind, and her determined teacher, Annie Sullivan, as they engage in a life-changing battle of wills. Through their remarkable journey, Annie relentlessly works to break through Helen's isolation and teach her to communicate, leading to a profound transformation that inspires and captivates audiences. This gripping tale of perseverance and the human spirit unfolds on the stage, showcasing the incredible strength of the bond between teacher and student.

Arthur Miller's fierce drama about an Italian-American longshoreman whose possessive love for his niece spirals into jealousy and betrayal in 1950s Brooklyn. This 2010 revival, directed by Gregory Mosher, stars Liev Schreiber and Scarlett Johansson.

Noel Coward's sparkling backstage comedy about a charismatic but self-absorbed actor fending off the amorous advances of starstruck admirers, a seductive young playwright, and his own tangled personal life. This 2010 revival at the American Airlines Theatre, directed by Nicholas Martin, stars Victor Garber.

A Little Night Music is a musical with music and lyrics by  Stephen Sondheim and book by Hugh Wheeler. Inspired by the 1955 Ingmar Bergman film Smiles of a Summer Night, it involves the romantic lives of several couples.

ct.n: Dan Moses Schreier and Gareth Owen

shows.syn: Race is a play by David Mamet that premiered on Broadway in December 2009. Mamet has stated that the intended "theme is race and the lies we tell each other on the subject.

What will it take to get two dozen powerfully passionate, exceedingly complicated, and all-too-human individuals to settle their differences, while they hold the very future of a nation in their hands? This Tony Award-winning Best Musical is tuneful, witty, and constantly surprising, especially in this revolutionary new production from directors Jeffrey L. Page and Diane Paulus. You may never think about our country—who we are and why—the same way again.

Based on the classic 1954 film starring Bing Crosby, this holiday musical follows two World War II veterans who put on a show to save a struggling Vermont inn. Featuring beloved Irving Berlin songs including "White Christmas," "Sisters," "Blue Skies," and "I Love a Piano."

In the Next Room (or The Vibrator Play) is a 2009 play by Sarah Ruhl, published by Samuel French. It concerns the early history of the vibrator, when doctors allegedly used it as a clinical device to bring women to orgasm as treatment for "hysteria.

===Act one=== Narration by J. Morgan, Henry Ford, Booker T. Washington, Emma Goldman, Harry Houdini, and Evelyn Nesbit connect three families: an upper-class White family from New Rochelle – the Little Boy (Edgar), his Father (who runs a fireworks factory), Mother, Mother's Younger Brother, and Grandfather; a Black couple from Harlem in New York City – Sarah, who adores the pianist Coalhouse Walker Jr.

ct.n: Robert Russell Bennett and Don Walker

shows.syn: The Royal Family is a play written by George S. Kaufman and Edna Ferber. Its premiere on Broadway was at the Selwyn Theatre on 28 December 1927, where it ran for 345 performances to close in October 1928.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre
shows.syn: In "Wishful Drinking," legendary actress Carrie Fisher takes the stage and delves into her extraordinary life, from growing up as the daughter of Hollywood royalty to navigating the complexities of addiction and mental health. With her signature wit and unflinching candor, she recounts the highs and lows of her remarkable journey, captivating audiences with her raw, honest, and often hilarious storytelling.

Chicago police officers Joey and Denny are longtime partners and best friends. Joey is single and lonely, and Denny is married with children, but both men have flaws and serious problems. Denny cares deeply for his family, and that provides the justification for his criminal actions. The two men offer contrasting descriptions to Internal Affairs interrogators of their harrowing experiences.

"Burn the Floor" is a heart-pounding dance extravaganza that takes the stage by storm. Audiences are whisked away on a journey of passion and rhythm, as a troupe of world-class dancers push the boundaries of ballroom and Latin dance. With electrifying choreography, pulsating music, and captivating performances, "Burn the Floor" ignites the stage, leaving audiences breathless and craving for more.

9 to 5: The Musical is a musical based on the 1980 film of the same name, with music and lyrics by Dolly Parton. It features a book by Patricia Resnick, based on the screenplay by Resnick and Colin Higgins.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre

shows.syn: In "The Norman Conquests: Table Manners," a dysfunctional family gathers for a weekend of unexpected chaos. As the eccentric characters navigate the complexities of their relationships, a web of secrets, desires, and comedic misunderstandings unfolds, creating a hilarious and poignant exploration of the human condition. Audiences are taken on a wild ride through the absurdities of everyday life, where the lines between propriety and impropriety blur, and the search for connection becomes a delightfully unpredictable adventure.

August Wilson's drama set in a Pittsburgh boarding house in 1911, where a mysterious man searches for his wife after years of forced servitude. Part of Wilson's acclaimed Pittsburgh Cycle chronicling the African-American experience decade by decade. This 2009 Bartlett Sher-directed revival played the Belasco Theatre.

From the Donmar Warehouse, Next to Normal is an intimate exploration of family and illness, loss and grief, featuring a thrilling rock score from composer Tom Kitt with book and lyrics by Brian Yorkey. At its heart is Diana—played by Olivier and GRAMMY Award nominee Caissie Levy—a suburban wife and mother living with bipolar disorder and haunted by her past.
Rock of Ages is an epithet that is used in some translations of Isaiah 26:4 and may refer to:

Irena's Vow tells the inspiring true story of a Polish Catholic woman who risks her life to shelter a Jewish family during the Nazi occupation of Poland. As the war rages on, Irena must navigate the dangerous and morally complex terrain of safeguarding her charges, testing the limits of her courage and her humanity in the face of unimaginable evil.

In the other plays of the "Berenger Cycle", Berenger appears as a depressed and insecure everyman who is prone to sentimentality. In Exit the King, he is the solipsistic and belligerent King Berenger the First who was apparently at one point able to command nature and force others to obey his will.

Before the play begins, two 11-year-old children, Ferdinand Reille and Bruno Vallon (Benjamin and Henry in the Broadway production), get involved in an argument because Bruno refuses to let Ferdinand join his 'gang'. Ferdinand knocks out two of Bruno's teeth with a stick. That night, the parents of both children meet to discuss the matter.

ct.n: Leonard Bernstein, Sid Ramin and Irwin Kostal

shows.syn: In "33 Variations," a modern-day musicologist, Katherine, becomes obsessed with Beethoven's mysterious fascination with a seemingly simple waltz. As she delves into the composer's creative process, the play intertwines her own struggles with a debilitating illness, unraveling the parallels between her life and Beethoven's. This captivating drama explores the power of artistic passion and the human drive to understand the unknown, drawing audiences into a poignant exploration of the creative spirit.
The Story of My Life is a musical with music and lyrics by Neil Bartram, and a book by Brian Hill.  The show follows two childhood friends from age six to 35 and has only two characters.

Hedda, the daughter of a general, has just returned to her villa in Kristiania (now Oslo) from her honeymoon. Her husband is George Tesman, a young, aspiring, and reliable academic who continued his research during their honeymoon. It becomes clear in the course of the play that she never loved him, but married him because she thinks her years of youthful abandon are over.

ct.n: Darron L. West and Bray Poor

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre

shows.syn: Dividing the Estate is a play by Horton Foote. The play premiered at the McCarter Theatre in 1989 and Off-Broadway in 2007, winning the Outer Critics Circle Award for Outstanding New Off-Broadway Play.

===Act I=== The play begins in the office of Hollywood producer Bobby Gould. Gould's longtime associate, Charlie Fox, has arrived with important news: movie star Doug Brown is interested in making a movie Fox had sent his way some time ago. Gould tells Fox about a book he has been asked to give a "courtesy read" to, meaning that it is not seriously being considered to be made into a film.

Arthur Miller's devastating drama about a wartime manufacturing scandal that tears apart a middle-class family, exposing the moral costs of profit and complicity. This 2008 production at the Gerald Schoenfeld Theatre is directed by Simon McBurney, starring John Lithgow, Dianne Wiest, and Patrick Wilson.

shows.t: To Be Or Not To Be
shows.syn: To Be or Not to Be is a play written by Nick Whitby, based on the 1942 film To Be or Not to Be. The play, described as a "black comedy"  was produced by the Manhattan Theatre Club, and ran on Broadway in 2008.

As Sir Thomas More refuses to recognize Henry VIII’s divorce and ascendancy as Supreme Head of the new Church of England, A Man for All Seasons reveals the risk of speaking truth to power and the clash that follows when fierce political will collides with deep moral conviction.

13 is a musical with music and lyrics by Jason Robert Brown and book by Dan Elish and Robert Horn.
Following a move from New York City to small-town Indiana, young Evan Goldman grapples with his parents' divorce, prepares for his impending Bar Mitzvah, and navigates the complicated social circles of a new school.

===Prologue=== The musical opens with Lucie Manette as a child, en route from France to England. She is delivered by Mr. Jarvis Lorry, an employee of Tellson's Bank to the home of Miss Pross, who had been nanny to Lucie's now-deceased mother. Later, Mr. Lorry returns to the Pross household to tell the now-adult Lucie that her father, Dr.

[title of show] is a one-act musical, with music and lyrics by Jeff Bowen and a book by Hunter Bell.  The show chronicles its own creation as an entry in the New York Musical Theatre Festival, and follows the struggles of the author and composer/lyricist and their two actress friends during the initial brief (three-week) creative period, along with subsequent events leading up to the show's production.

Bernard is a swinging bachelor in 1960s Paris juggling three flight attendant fiancées, each unaware of the others. When his friend Robert visits, the delicate balance of Bernard's carefully orchestrated love life begins to unravel in hilarious fashion, as the women's flight schedules start to overlap and the plot twists and turns in this fast-paced, door-slamming comedy.

Tony Award® winner, Emmy Award® winner, and Oscar® nominee Laurence Fishburne is both the star and the playwright of this one-man tour de force which he describes as “the stories and lies people have told me. And that I have told myself.”

The Country Girl is a 1950 dramatic play by American playwright Clifford Odets which was subsequently adapted as a film of the same name in 1954, starring Bing Crosby and Grace Kelly.
Uta Hagen played the title role of Georgie Elgin in its original production, with Paul Kelly as her husband Frank, and Steven Hill as theatre director Bernie Dodd.

In Baltimore 1954, Wade "Cry-Baby" Walker, known for his ability to shed a single tear, leads a group of "drapes". One day after school, Allison Vernon-Williams, a beautiful upper-class girl tired of being a "square", approaches Cry-Baby, and the two fall in love. That same day, he interrupts a talent show at the R.

===Act I=== On a South Pacific island during World War II, two half-Polynesian children, Ngana and Jerome, happily sing as they play together ("Dites-Moi"). Ensign Nellie Forbush, a naïve U. Navy nurse from Little Rock, Arkansas, has fallen in love with Emile de Becque, a middle-aged French plantation owner, though she has known him only briefly.

===Act I=== Baby June and Louise, the two daughters of Rose Hovick, play the vaudeville circuit around the United States in the early 1920s. Rose, the archetype of a stage mother, is aggressive and domineering, pushing her children to perform. While June is an extroverted, talented child star, the older girl, Louise, is shy.

shows.t: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof
shows.syn: as Maggie in the original Broadway production of Cat on a Hot Tin Roof (1955)]] A family in the American South is in crisis, especially the husband and wife, Brick and Margaret (usually called Maggie or "Maggie the Cat"), and the crisis unspools with Brick's family over the course of one evening's gathering at the family plantation in Mississippi.

shows.t: Sunday in the Park with George
shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre
shows.syn: Stephen Sondheim and James Lapine's Pulitzer Prize-winning musical about art, connection, and creation. Inspired by Georges Seurat's pointillist painting "A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte," the show explores the sacrifice and passion behind artistic vision. This 2008 revival at the Roundabout is directed by Sam Buntrock.

In "Come Back, Little Sheba," a middle-aged couple's quiet lives are upended when a young, attractive boarder moves in. As tensions rise and secrets are uncovered, the couple confronts the realities of their unfulfilled dreams and the consequences of their past choices. This poignant drama explores the complex dynamics of relationships, the power of desire, and the pursuit of happiness in the face of life's disappointments.
Based on one of Hans Christian Andersen’s most beloved stories and the classic animated film, Disney’s The Little Mermaid is a hauntingly beautiful love story for the ages.

After having lived in the United States for six years, Teddy brings his wife, Ruth, home for the first time to meet his working-class family in North London, where he grew up, and which she finds more familiar than their academic life in America. The two married in London before moving to the United States.

Is He Dead? is a play by Mark Twain based on his earlier 1893 short story. The play, written by Twain in 1898, was first published in print in 2003 after Mark Twain scholar Shelley Fisher Fishkin read the manuscript in the archives of the Mark Twain Papers at the University of California at Berkeley.

After losing yet another job, Sharky has returned home to Dublin to build a new, sober existence with his cantankerous elder brother, Richard, recently blinded in a drunken accident. But it’s Christmas Eve, and the drinks are flowing as old friends convene for an annual game of poker. This year, an immortal stranger from Sharky’s past arrives, raising the stakes to eternal consequence.

In 1921, 14-year-old genius Philo Farnsworth moves with his family from Indian Creek, Utah to Rigby, Idaho to live on a potato farm owned by Farnsworth's uncle. While plowing a field, he has an idea for a way to transmit images electronically over the air, which he explains to his ninth grade basic science teacher.

ct.n: Walter Trarbach and Tony Smolenski IV
Tony Smolenski IV and Walter Trarbach

shows.syn: Early in the 20th century, Frederick Frankenstein, a lecturing physician at an American medical school, is actively distancing himself from his grandfather Victor Frankenstein, the infamous mad scientist, pronouncing his surname as "Fronkensteen". When Frederick inherits the family castle in Transylvania, he travels to Europe to inspect the property.

Edmond Rostand's swashbuckling romance about a brilliant, big-nosed poet-soldier who ghostwrites love letters for a handsome but tongue-tied cadet wooing the woman they both adore. This 2007 production at the Richard Rodgers Theatre, directed by David Leveaux, stars Kevin Kline.

In 1960, nine-year-old Calogero lives in Belmont, a working-class Italian-American neighborhood in the Bronx with his father Lorenzo, a Surface Transit (later MTA) bus driver, and his mother Rosina. Calogero becomes enamored of the Mafia presence in his neighborhood, led by local boss Sonny. One day, Calogero witnesses Sonny shoot and kill a man who was assaulting his friend.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre
shows.syn: In the vibrant world of 1970s New York, a down-on-his-luck Manhattanite stumbles into a bathhouse, expecting a simple escape. Instead, he finds himself embroiled in a hilarious case of mistaken identity, entangled with a flamboyant mobster and a bevy of colorful characters. As he navigates this unexpected adventure, he discovers the power of self-acceptance and the joy of embracing the unexpected.

Because of changes to the musical that have been made since the 1978 film adaptation, several variants exist. In the event two songs are listed at any given point, the first is from the 1972 Off-Broadway version, and the second is from revivals that use the film music, such as the 2007 version. ===Act I=== At revivals that use the 1978 song "Grease", it is typically inserted at or near the beginning of the show.

It is 1980 and chalk artist Sonny Malone is dissatisfied with his sidewalk mural of the Greek Muses (daughters of Zeus) and determines to kill himself. On Mount Olympus, Clio (pronounced "Kleye-o"), the youngest, perkiest Muse, convinces her six sisters (two of whom are men in drag), to travel to Venice Beach (rising out of the sidewalk mural) to inspire Sonny ("I'm Alive").

ct.n: T. Richard Fitzgerald and Carl Casella

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre
shows.syn: ===Act I=== It's the Fourth of July in 1936, in the small town of Three Point in the Southwestern U. , where a blistering heat wave has the local sheriff, File, and the other townsfolk forever eyeing the sky ("Another Hot Day"). Elsewhere in town, on the ranch of widower H. Curry, the air is also charged with anticipation, due to the imminent arrival of H.

===Act one=== The members of UCLA's sorority Delta Nu congratulate their president, Elle Woods; Elle is expecting her boyfriend, Warner Huntington III, to propose marriage to her later that night. Led by Margot, Serena, and Pilar, the girls help Elle find the perfect dress for the occasion ("Omigod You Guys").

"Inherit the Wind" is a gripping courtroom drama that delves into the clash between science and religion. As a small town is rocked by a controversial trial over the teaching of evolution, two powerful attorneys engage in a battle of wits and ideologies, igniting a fierce debate that challenges the very foundations of beliefs. With compelling characters and a thought-provoking narrative, the play explores the timeless struggle between progress and tradition, inviting audiences to examine the limits of freedom and the pursuit of truth.

Set in a dilapidated Connecticut house in early September 1923, the play focuses on three characters: Josie, a domineering Irish woman with a quick tongue and a ruined reputation, her conniving father, tenant farmer and bullying widower Phil Hogan, and James Tyrone, Jr. , Hogan's landlord and drinking companion, a cynical alcoholic haunted by the death of his mother.

===Act I=== In 1959, a new musical called "Robbin' Hood! ", a western version of Robin Hood, is in the midst of its preview performances at the Colonial Theatre in Boston, Massachusetts. During the closing number of one of the performances, Madame Marian, played by faded film star diva Jessica Cranshaw, looks on as Rob Hood, played by Bobby Pepper, wins a sharp-shooting contest and proposes to Miss Nancy, a schoolmarm, played by Niki Harris.

In "Prelude to a Kiss," a chance encounter between a young bride and an elderly stranger leads to a supernatural body swap. As the newlyweds grapple with the mystical transformation, they must confront the complexities of love, identity, and the true meaning of commitment. This enchanting play explores the delicate balance between the physical and the spiritual, challenging audiences to question the nature of human connection.

=== Act I === Monday, 18 March 1918: evening. In the British trenches facing Saint-Quentin, Captain Hardy converses with Lieutenant Osborne, an older man and public school master, who has come to relieve him. Hardy jokes about the behaviour of Captain Stanhope, who has turned to alcohol to cope with the stress that the war has caused him.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre
shows.syn: The Apple Tree is a series of three musical playlets with music by Jerry Bock, lyrics by Sheldon Harnick, and a book by Bock and Harnick with contributions from Jerome Coopersmith. Each act has its own storyline, but all three are tied together by a common theme (be careful what you wish for) and common references, such as references to the color brown.

===Act I=== Wendla Bergmann, an adolescent in late 19th-century Germany, laments that her mother gave her "no way to handle things" and has not taught her the lessons she is meant to know as a young woman ("Mama Who Bore Me"). She tells her mother that it is time she learned where babies come from, considering that she is about to be an aunt for the second time.

; Act I The Last Real Record Store – Rob, Pale Young Men, Dick, Barry Desert Island Top 5 Break-Ups – Rob, Top 5 Girls It's No Problem – Dick She Goes – Liz, Rob Ian's Here – Ian, Laura Number Five with a Bullet – Laura, Top 5 Girls Ready to Settle – Marie, Back-Up Singer Terrible Things – Marie The Last Real Record Store (Reprise) – Barry, Dick, Rob, Pale Young Men Nine Percent Chance – Rob, Barry, Dick, Pale Young Men ; Act II I Slept with Someone – Rob, Laura Exit Sign – Neil Cryin' in the...

In "The Vertical Hour," a former war correspondent, Nadia Blye, finds her personal and political beliefs challenged when she meets a British doctor in the English countryside. As their passionate debate on the Iraq War unfolds, the boundaries between the personal and the political become increasingly blurred, leading to a thought-provoking exploration of the complexities of morality, loyalty, and the consequences of our choices.
Trade in the dull gray of the concrete jungle for the glamour of 19th-century Paris. Be immersed in a world inspired by the Belle Époque, a period in which the arts flourished and spirits were high.

"The Coast of Utopia [Part 1 - Voyage]" takes audiences on a captivating journey through the lives of 19th-century Russian intellectuals, as they grapple with the pursuit of philosophical ideals and the ever-changing political landscape. Weaving together a tapestry of personal relationships, ideological debates, and the quest for a better world, this gripping play invites viewers to explore the complexities of human nature and the power of ideas to shape a nation's destiny.
Discover the supercalifragilisticexpialidocious story of a magical, common-sensical nanny who arrives at one family’s home and opens their eyes to a world of adventure right under our noses.

"The Little Dog Laughed" is a witty, modern play that explores the complexities of fame, sexuality, and the entertainment industry. When a rising Hollywood star's secret relationship with a male escort threatens to derail his career, his conniving agent and the escort's girlfriend are forced to confront their own desires and the consequences of their choices. With sharp dialogue and unexpected twists, the play delves into the power dynamics that shape both personal and professional lives in the pursuit of success.

===Prologue=== In 1815 France, Jean Valjean, "Prisoner 24601", is released on parole by guard Javert after 19 years hard labor in prison for stealing bread ("Work Song (Look Down)"). Marked by his yellow ticket of leave ("On Parole"), Valjean is shunned and cannot find decent work until the Bishop of Digne offers him shelter.

ct.n: Jon Weston, Andrew Bruce and Autograph

shows.syn: |productions = }} Grey Gardens is a musical with book by Doug Wright, music by Scott Frankel, and lyrics by Michael Korie, produced in 2006 and based on the 1975 documentary of the same title about the lives of Edith Ewing Bouvier Beale ("Big Edie") and her daughter Edith Bouvier Beale ("Little Edie") by Albert and David Maysles.

In the eccentric Heartbreak House, a diverse group of aristocrats, artists, and eccentrics gather, each harbouring their own secrets and desires. As they engage in a delightful dance of wit, romance, and social commentary, the impending threat of war looms, forcing them to confront the fragility of their privileged existence. Filled with Shavian wisdom and biting humor, this captivating play by George Bernard Shaw examines the human condition with piercing insight and unforgettable charm.

The show opens during an audition for an upcoming Broadway production. The formidable director Zach and his assistant choreographer Larry put the 24 dancers through their paces. Every dancer is desperate for work ("I Hope I Get It"). After a round of cuts, 17 dancers remain. Zach tells them he is looking for a strong eight-member dancing chorus of four boys and four girls.

ct.n: Jonathan Tunick, Bill Byers and Hershy Kay

shows.syn: Conor McPherson’s acclaimed Irish play, Shining City, is a gripping Dublin-based ghost story. It follows priest-turned-therapist, Ian, and his tortured widower patient, John, who holds a terrible secret. Together they unpack John’s winding story, leaving them, and those around them, altered in surprising and unforeseen ways.

In the midst of World War II, a young naval officer stands accused of mutiny, sparking a gripping legal battle that challenges the very foundations of military discipline. As the court-martial unfolds, tensions rise and reputations are put on the line, as the true nature of leadership and the limits of obedience are tested in this riveting courtroom drama.

In "Faith Healer," a charismatic, enigmatic healer named Frank Grace travels the remote corners of the British Isles, accompanied by his loyal wife and embittered manager, each bearing a different perspective on his enigmatic powers. As they recount their intertwined stories, a captivating portrait of the nature of faith, truth, and the human condition emerges, leaving audiences pondering the line between healing and deception.

===Act I=== Man in Chair, a mousy, agoraphobic Broadway fanatic, seeking to cure his "non-specific sadness", listens to a recording of the fictional 1928 musical comedy, The Drowsy Chaperone. As he listens to this rare recording, the characters appear in his dingy apartment, and it is transformed into an impressive Broadway set with seashell footlights, sparkling furniture, painted backdrops, and glitzy costumes.

In 1985, Robbie Hart works as a wedding singer in Ridgefield, New Jersey, and is preparing to marry his fiancée, Linda. At the reception hall where he performs, he befriends Julia Sullivan, a new waitress. Robbie agrees to sing at Julia's wedding, though her fiancé, businessman and bond investor Glenn Gulia, has not yet set a date.

This synopsis reflects the final, New York version. ;Act I Lestat leaves his mother Gabrielle to go to Paris to live with his boyfriend Nicolas ("My Beautiful Boy"). He finds Nicolas working as an actor in a small theater, happy with his life ("In Paris"). They go to Nicolas' flat and it becomes clear they're in love with each other ("The Bugs and the Bears").

The History Boys is a play by British playwright Alan Bennett. The play premiered at the Royal National Theatre in London's West End on 18 May 2004. Its Broadway debut was on 23 April 2006 at the Broadhurst Theatre where 185 performances were staged before it closed on 1 October 2006.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre

shows.syn: Walker and his sister Nan met in an unoccupied studio in lower Manhattan in 1995. Walker, who had disappeared the day after his father's funeral, now months later is living in this apartment where his father, Ned Janeway and business partner, Theo Wexler, once lived and worked designing the famous "Janeway House".

ct.n: Peter John Still and Marc Salzberg

shows.syn: As in the original movie, Festen satirises the hypocrisy of a large and wealthy family by observing the events that unfold at the ancestral home during a reunion held to celebrate the oldest family member's 60th birthday. As the time arises for birthday speeches to be made to the party's subject, one of his sons stands and asks the assembled guests to choose which of two prepared speeches he should read.

"Barefoot in the Park" is a delightful romantic comedy that follows the newlywed couple, Corie and Paul, as they navigate the challenges of adjusting to their first home, a tiny New York City apartment. With Corie's free-spirited nature clashing with Paul's cautious sensibilities, the pair must learn to compromise and embrace the unexpected joys of married life. As they encounter an array of colorful characters, including Corie's eccentric mother and their quirky neighbor, the young couple's love is put to the test in this charming and heartwarming tale.

Rabbit Hole is a play written by David Lindsay-Abaire. It was the recipient of the 2007 Pulitzer Prize for Drama. The play premiered on Broadway in 2006, and it has also been produced by regional theatres in cities such as Los Angeles, Philadelphia and Pittsburgh. The play had its Spanish language premiere in San Juan, Puerto Rico in the autumn of 2010.

The show is divided into thematic sections during which "Chita" (Chita Rivera) shares anecdotes from her life. Members of the ensemble play the part of various figures in her life. ;Act I Chita remembers her father, a professional saxophonist who died when she was seven years old, playing "Perfidia" for her.

shows.v: Harold and Miriam Steinberg Center for Theatre

shows.syn: In Edward Albee's Seascape, a couple's tranquil beach vacation is upended when they encounter a pair of mysterious, humanoid sea creatures. As the two couples navigate their unexpected encounter, they grapple with questions of evolution, communication, and the very nature of humanity. Blending wry humor and profound insights, Seascape explores the boundaries between the familiar and the unfamiliar, inviting audiences to consider their place in the grand tapestry of life.

On a midnight train trip on the way to Limmeridge House as a drawing teacher, Walter Hartright sees a strange woman dressed entirely in white, apparently fleeing from someone and desperate to share a terrible secret with him. The signalman is scared because although he saw no-one, it was predicted a year earlier that in a 'year to this day', someone would be found dead on the railway track ("Prologue").

JERSEY BOYS is the behind-the-music story of Frankie Valli and The Four Seasons. They were just four guys from Jersey, until they sang their very first note. They had a sound nobody had ever heard… and the radio just couldn’t get enough of. But while their harmonies were perfect on stage, off stage it was a very different story -- a story that has made them an international sensation all over again.

Sweeney Todd is a fictional character who first appeared as the villain of the penny dreadful serial The String of Pearls (1846–1847). The original tale became a feature of 19th-century melodrama and London legend. A barber from Fleet Street, Todd murders his customers with a straight razor and gives their corpses to Mrs.

In My Life was a 2005 Broadway musical with music, lyrics, and book by Joseph Brooks.
Described by Playbill as being "generally regarded" to be "one of the strangest shows ever to have graced a Broadway stage", it told the story of a romance between a journalist with obsessive compulsive disorder and a singer-songwriter with Tourette's syndrome and later a brain tumor; as they fall in love, they are observed by an angel in Heaven who decides that their story would make a great "reality opera".

Absurd Person Singular is a 1972 play by Alan Ayckbourn. Divided into three acts, it documents the changing fortunes of three married couples. Each act takes place at a Christmas celebration at one of the couples' homes on successive Christmas Eves.

shows.t: A Naked Girl on the Appian Way
shows.syn: "A Naked Girl on the Appian Way" takes audiences on a thrilling journey into the lives of a seemingly ordinary suburban family whose world is turned upside down when a mysterious naked woman appears on their doorstep. As they grapple with the shocking revelation and its unexpected consequences, the family is forced to confront their deepest secrets and the fragility of their relationships, leading to a gripping and emotionally charged theatrical experience.

ct.n: David Van Tieghem and Jill B.C. Du Boff

shows.syn: Mark Twain Tonight! is a one-man play devised by Hal Holbrook, in which he depicted Mark Twain giving a dramatic recitation selected from several of Twain's writings, with an emphasis on the comic ones.

shows.t: After the Night and the Music
shows.syn: After the Night and the Music is a one-act play in three parts, written by Elaine May. It opened in 2005. May derived the name of the play from the Howard Dietz song "You and the Night and the Music".

shows.t: The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee
shows.syn: A hilarious musical comedy following six quirky adolescents competing in a spelling bee, with audience members pulled on stage to compete alongside them.
The untold story of the Witches of Oz. Long before Dorothy arrives, Elphaba and Glinda meet as sorcery students and form an unlikely friendship that will change the land of Oz forever.
A comedy musical featuring puppets and human actors exploring the challenges of adulthood in New York City, inspired by Sesame Street but decidedly for grown-ups.
Set in 1960s Baltimore, plus-size teen Tracy Turnblad pursues her dream of dancing on a local TV show while fighting for racial integration.
Mel Brooks' record-breaking musical comedy about two theatrical producers who scheme to get rich by overselling shares in a guaranteed Broadway flop called "Springtime for Hitler."
Disney's award-winning musical brings the African savanna to Broadway with stunning puppetry and masks. Young Simba must embrace his destiny to become king after tragedy strikes the Pride Lands.

Set in the legendary city during the roaring twenties, Chicago tells the story of Roxie Hart, a housewife who murders her lover and convinces her husband to take the fall. The longest-running American musical in Broadway history.
Jonathan Larson's Pulitzer Prize-winning rock musical loosely based on Puccini's La Boheme, following a group of young artists and musicians struggling to survive in New York's East Village.
Andrew Lloyd Webber's iconic musical about a mysterious masked figure who haunts the Paris Opera House and becomes obsessed with a beautiful soprano.

ct.n: Richard Stilgoe and Andrew Lloyd Webber

shows.syn: The epic sung-through musical based on Victor Hugo's novel, following ex-convict Jean Valjean as he seeks redemption in 19th-century France amid revolution and upheaval.

Andrew Lloyd Webber's musical based on T.S. Eliot's Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats, in which a tribe of Jellicle cats gather for their annual ball to decide which cat will ascend to the Heaviside Layer.

|premiere_location = Shubert Theatre, Chicago |productions= 2002 Chicago 2002 Broadway 2004 North American Tour 2006 West End |awards= |Choreography=Twyla Tharp}} Movin' Out is a 2002 jukebox musical featuring the songs of Billy Joel. Conceived and created by Twyla Tharp, the musical tells the story of a generation of American youth growing up on Long Island during the 1960s and their experiences with the Vietnam War.

shows.syn: ===Act I=== Guido Contini, a famous Italian film director, has turned forty and faces double crises: he has to shoot a film for which he can't write the script, and his wife of twenty years, the film star Luisa del Forno, may be about to leave him if he can't pay more attention to their marriage. Luisa's efforts to talk to him are drowned out by voices in his head: voices of women in his life whom he has loved ("Overture delle Donne").

===Act I=== Officer Lockstock, a policeman, grimly welcomes the audience, assisted by the street urchin Little Sally. According to the pair, a twenty-year drought has caused a terrible water shortage, making private toilets unthinkable. All restroom activities are done in public toilets (Public Amenities) controlled by a megacorporation called "Urine Good Company" (referred to as the UGC).

Nothing On is the type of farce in which young women run about in their underwear, men drop their trousers, and many doors continually bang open and shut.

The Elephant Man opens with Frederick Treves, an up-and-coming surgeon, meeting his new employer Francis Carr-Gomm, the administrator of the London Hospital. Ross, the manager of a freak show, invites a crowd on Whitechapel Road to come view John Merrick, the Elephant Man. Treves happens upon the freak show and is intrigued by Merrick's disorder.

Thoroughly Modern Millie is a musical with music by Jeanine Tesori, lyrics by Dick Scanlan, and a book by Richard Morris and Scanlan. It is based on the 1967 film of the same name, which itself was based on the British musical Chrysanthemum, which opened in London in 1956. Millie soon begins to take delight in the flapper lifestyle, but problems arise when she checks into a hotel owned by the leader of a white slavery ring in China.

A film adaptation was released in 1955.

"Beauty and the Beast" is a fairy tale written by the French novelist Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve and published anonymously in 1740 in La Jeune Américaine et les contes marins (The Young American and Marine Tales).
Villeneuve's original story was abridged, revised, and published by French novelist Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont in 1756 in Magasin des enfants (Children's Collection) which became the most commonly retold version.

===Act I=== A pantomime of never-ceasing activities depicts the hustle and bustle of New York City ("Runyonland"). Three small-time gamblers, Nicely-Nicely Johnson, Benny Southstreet, and Rusty Charlie, argue over which horse will win a big race ("Fugue for Tinhorns"). The band members of the Save-a-Soul Mission, led by the pious and beautiful Sergeant Sarah Brown, call for sinners to "Follow the Fold" and repent ("Follow the Fold").

He is reluctantly guided by the mysterious 'Chimney Man,' who forces him to recall the more painful moments of his life when he attempts to ignore or embellish them. When his Creole grandmother discovers his new lifestyle, she disowns him.

Marvin explains the situation: he has left his wife Trina for a man, Whizzer, but no one is happy with his attempts to integrate Whizzer into the family ("A Tight-Knit Family").

===Act I=== Tevye is a poor Jewish milkman in Czarist Russia. He and his bossy, sharp-tongued wife Golde have five daughters (eldest to youngest): Tzeitel, Hodel, Chava, Shprintze and Bielke. Tevye explains the customs of the Jews in the Russian shtetl of Anatevka in 1905, where their lives are as precarious as the perch of a fiddler on a roof ("Tradition").

The real-life scenes feature full-color sets and costumes, while the movie scenes are in black-and-white. With the exception of the actors playing Stine and Stone, most of the cast doubles as characters in the "real" world and their fictional counterparts.

The Life is a musical with a book by David Newman, Ira Gasman and Cy Coleman, music by Coleman, and lyrics by Gasman.
Based on an original idea by Gasman, the show explores the underbelly of Times Square's 42nd Street, inhabited by pimps and prostitutes, druggies and dealers, and runaways and street people in the era prior to its Disneyfication.

Jack wishes his cow, Milky White, would give milk, and his Mother wishes that Jack would sell Milky White. A Baker and his Wife wish to have a child, and Little Red Riding Hood wishes for bread to bring to her ill Grandmother.

The first act introduces the main character, René Gallimard, a civil servant attached to the French embassy in China. In a prison, Gallimard is serving a sentence for treason. Through a series of flashbacks and imagined conversations, Gallimard tells an audience his story about a woman that he loved and lost.

Police officers Krupke and Lt. Schrank warn them to stop fighting on their beat. The police chase the Sharks off, and then the Jets plan how they can assure their continued dominance of the street.

He loves spectacle and excitement, using hype and "humbug" to promote his exhibits. His wife Charity disagrees with his use of "humbug" but loves him and aims to keep him grounded.

shows.syn: ===Act I=== The young woman Charity Hope Valentine is a taxi dancer at a dance hall called the Fandango Ballroom in New York City. With a shoulder bag and a heart tattooed on her left shoulder, Charity meets her boyfriend Charlie in Central Park. While Charlie silently preens himself, Charity speaks the pick-up lines she imagines him saying, and tells him how handsome he is ("You Should See Yourself").

shows.t: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof

shows.syn: ===Act I=== Tevye is a poor Jewish milkman in Czarist Russia. He and his bossy, sharp-tongued wife Golde have five daughters (eldest to youngest): Tzeitel, Hodel, Chava, Shprintze and Bielke. Tevye explains the customs of the Jews in the Russian shtetl of Anatevka in 1905, where their lives are as precarious as the perch of a fiddler on a roof ("Tradition").

In the mid-1990s, the once-successful steel mills of Sheffield, South Yorkshire have shut down and most of the workers have been made redundant. Former steelworkers Gary "Gaz" Schofield and Dave Horsfall resort to stealing scrap metal from the abandoned mills and selling it to make money, taking Gaz's son Nathan with them for assistance, but a security guard keeps surprising them and locking them inside the mill.

Setting: New York City, late 1950s ACT ONE Ella Peterson works for "Susanswerphone", a telephone answering service owned by a woman named Sue. She listens in on others' lives and adds some interest to her own humdrum existence by adopting different identities – and voices – for her clients. They include Blake Barton, an out-of-work Method actor, Dr.

shows.syn: ===Act I=== Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve Apostles, worries that the followers of Jesus are getting out of control and may be seen as a threat by the Roman Empire, who might harshly suppress them ("Heaven on Their Minds"). The other apostles anticipate entering Jerusalem alongside Jesus and ask him about his plans, but Jesus tells them not to worry about the future.

===Act I=== Georges, the master of ceremonies, welcomes the audience to his St.  Tropez drag nightclub, "La Cage aux Folles". The chorus line known as Les Cagelles appear and introduce themselves to the audience ("We Are What We Are"). Georges and his spouse, Albin, have lived happily together for many years in an apartment above La Cage with their "maid" Jacob.

The story follows Zorba as he talks his way into traveling with Nikos, a young American who has inherited an abandoned mine on Crete. Eventually, Zorba falls in love with a French woman, Hortense. Meanwhile, Nikos falls in love with The Widow. Tragedy ensues when Zorba loses all of his money to a belly dancer.

===Act I=== Setting: a Chinese restaurant Scene 1: Shelly Levene tries to convince office manager John Williamson to give him some of "the Glengarry leads" (names and phone numbers of promising potential clients for expensive properties). Williamson is willing to sell some of the prime leads, but demands cash in advance.

shows.t: Sunday in the Park with George
shows.syn: ===Act I=== In 1884, Georges Seurat, known as George in the musical, is sketching studies for his painting A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte. He announces to the audience, "White, a blank page or canvas. The challenge: bring order to the whole, through design, composition, tension, balance, light and harmony.

Torch Song Trilogy is a collection of three plays by Harvey Fierstein rendered in three acts: International Stud, Fugue in a Nursery, and Widows and Children First! The story centers on Arnold Beckoff, a Jewish homosexual, drag queen, and torch singer who lives in New York City in the late 1970s and early 1980s.

Anna Christie is the story of a former prostitute who falls in love, but runs into difficulty in turning her life around. === Act I === The first act takes place in a bar owned by Johnny the Priest and tended by Larry. Coal-barge captain Old Chris receives a letter from his daughter, a young woman he has not seen since he lived in Sweden with his family and she was five years old.

===Act 1=== In 1933 New York City, eleven-year-old Annie sleeps in an orphanage, with many other girls her age. When six-year-old Molly wakes up from a bad dream, Annie comforts her by singing about her own parents; even though they abandoned her at the orphanage as a baby, she holds on to the hope that they will come back for her ("Maybe").

On the Town is a musical with music by Leonard Bernstein and book and lyrics by Betty Comden and Adolph Green, based on Jerome Robbins' idea for his  1944 ballet Fancy Free, which he had set to Bernstein's music. The musical introduced several popular and classic songs, among them "New York, New York", "Lonely Town", "I Can Cook, Too" (for which Bernstein also wrote the lyric), and "Some Other Time".

shows.t: Midnight In The Garden Of Good And Evil
shows.syn: Based on a bestselling book, *Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil* is a new musical about how a community in Savannah, Georgia responds to a local murder. Expect plenty of Southern charm — and even more Southern secrets.
*Dreamgirls* is a story of fame, and the musical journey, through love and loss, that guides three women toward it.

;Act 1 Following a brief courtship, Elyot and Sibyl are honeymooning at a hotel in Deauville, France, although her curiosity about his first marriage is not helping his romantic mood. In the adjoining suite Amanda and Victor are starting their new life together, although he cannot stop thinking of the cruelty Amanda's ex-husband displayed towards her.

shows.t: 10 Things I Hate About You on Broadway

shows.syn: *Dolly: An Original Musical* follows the life and career of a true music star, Dolly Parton. See, and hear, her story as you never have before in this new jukebox musical written by the icon herself.

===Act I=== In 1865, Josephine March (Jo) receives a notice of rejection from a publisher; it is her twenty-second rejection. Jo asks Professor Bhaer, another boarder at Mrs. Kirk's boarding house, his opinion on her story ("An Operatic Tragedy"). The professor is not impressed by her story, telling her that he thinks that she can write something better.

shows.v: The Irene Diamond Stage at the Pershing Square Signature Center
shows.syn: *The Receptionist* is a wry and dark play about the complicity of evil, set in a modern office.

Heartfelt musical comedy about love, family, and drag, directed by Robert O’Hara. Set on the glittering French Riviera, this Tony Award–winning musical follows Georges and Albin, longtime partners who run a drag nightclub in St. Tropez. Their world is turned upside down when their son, Jean-Michel, announces his engagement to the daughter of conservative parents and invites them to dinner. This Encores! presentation revives the original 1983 orchestrations by Jerry Herman, heard live for the first time since the show’s Broadway debut. The creative team includes book writer Harvey Fierstein and composer-lyricist Jerry Herman, both Lifetime Achievement Tony Award recipients. Produced by New York City Center Encores!, the limited-run staging continues the company’s mission to celebrate and...
*Mexodus* is a groundbreaking hip-hop musical about people who escaped slavery by moving across the Mexican border.

Relive all the funniest moments from the classic sitcom at this musical parody. See Jerry, George, Elaine, and Kramer's most iconic adventures set to hilarious songs like "The Master of their Domain," "Seasons of George," and "The Yadda Yadda Song."

A revenge tragedy centered on a Roman general whose victory ignites a cycle of brutality. The play follows Titus Andronicus, Rome’s most celebrated general, whose victory over the Goths sets off a relentless cycle of retaliation. After Titus sacrifices a member of the defeated enemy, Queen Tamora and her lover Aaron the Moor orchestrate a series of brutal reprisals that engulf Titus’s family and destabilize Rome itself. What begins as a triumph of state power descends into escalating violence, exposing how vengeance corrodes personal, political, and moral order. Written early in his career, Titus Andronicus is widely regarded as William Shakespeare’s bloodiest work. This production is directed by Jesse Berger and stars Patrick Page as Titus, one of Shakespeare’s most demanding tragic ro...

shows.v: The Griffin Theater at The Shed
shows.syn: The Other Place is a play by American playwright Sharr White. The play premiered Off-Broadway in 2011 and then ran on Broadway.
*The Unknown* is a thrilling new play about a writer on the edge.
*Kenrex* is a true crime drama about Ken McElroy, a man from Missouri convicted of second-degree assault.

shows.t: Entangled: 12 Scenes in a Circle K off the I-40 in New Mexico

shows.syn: Be there for this musical spoof on one of the most beloved sitcoms of all time. Go on an adventure with Chandler, Rachel, Phoebe, Ross, Monica, and Joey and hear all the iconic moments from TV set to hilarious parody songs.

When a nerdy flower shop worker finds a mysterious talking plant, it completely changes his life — but at a bloody cost. Go down to Skid Row for the latest revival of Alan Menken and Howard Ashman's horror comedy blossoming off Broadway.

shows.v: Atlantic Theater Company - Linda Gross Theater
shows.syn: *The Reservoir* is a new comedy about getting sober, getting your life together, and getting back into the rhythms of living in your hometown.
*Night Side Songs* is a communal musical experience in which actors play out the stories of healthcare workers and patients.
*Heathers* is a musical comedy based on the cult film and follows Veronica as she tries to navigate teenage issues, first love, and three Heathers who rule the school with an iron fist.
*Mother Russia* is a sharp, dark comedy about a St. Petersburg spy who falls in love with the woman he is meant to track: an edgy artist with a mysterious past.

Ulysses is a 1705 tragedy by the British writer Nicholas Rowe. Rowe turned back to writing tragedies following his unsuccessful comedy The Biter of the previous year. The cast included Thomas Betterton as Ulysses, Barton Booth as Telemachus, Elizabeth Barry as Penelope and Anne Bracegirdle as Semanthe.

A world-premiere political drama directed by Doug Hughes. Set across decades of personal and political transformation, Public Charge follows Julissa, who immigrates from the Dominican Republic to the United States at age seven. After rising from a Wall Street law practice to serve as a senior official in the U.S. Department of State under Secretary Hillary Clinton, she confronts the human and moral complexities of public service—deploying aid to earthquake-stricken Haiti, navigating immigration politics, and negotiating the release of a wrongfully imprisoned American in Cuba. The play is co-written by former U.S. Ambassador Julissa Reynoso and award-winning playwright Michael J. Chepiga (The Rape of the Sabine Women, by Grace B. Matthias). Drawing from Reynoso’s firsthand experience in ...

Join Queenie's wild, gin-soaked 1920s party where desire and danger collide in a jazz-age tragedy. Set in the hedonistic world of late 1920s New York, The Wild Party follows Queenie, a vaudeville performer, and her volatile lover Burrs as they host a raucous party that spirals into chaos. Amid the haze of jazz, gin, and jealousy, the night exposes a crowd of guests chasing escape and excitement, only to confront bitter truths by morning. The musical is based on Joseph Moncure March’s once-banned narrative poem, whose raw depiction of excess and disillusionment shocked readers of its time. The production reunites composer and lyricist Michael John LaChiusa (The Gardens of Anuncia, Hello Again) and director George C. Wolfe (Jelly’s Last Jam) in a revival directed by Lili-Anne Brown, a two...

The Adding Machine is a 1923 play by Elmer Rice.
The 2007 Columbia Encyclopedia of Modern Drama called it "a landmark of American Expressionism, reflecting the growing interest in this highly subjective and nonrealistic form of modern drama.

OnlyFans star attempts 1,000-subscriber stunt, exploring sex complexities via documentary maker. The play centers on Pollie, an OnlyFans creator who seeks significant fame by attempting an extreme public stunt: engaging sexually with 1,000 of her subscribers during the Edinburgh Fringe Festival. This premise reflects a current cultural climate where sex work is framed as highly competitive, leading to attention-seeking, extreme actions by creators vying for dominance in an oversaturated online market. The narrative acknowledges the recent viral nature of claims regarding high "body counts" and the resulting international debate among liberal feminists. The story is filtered through the perspective of an empathetic Documentary Maker, drawing focus to Pollie's complex relationship with se...
*Blood/Love* is a new pop opera about a vampire who falls in love with a mortal.
John Thomas Harding is an American country music songwriter. He has written songs for Uncle Kracker, Kenny Chesney, Keith Urban, Blake Shelton, Dierks Bentley, and Darius Rucker.

A comic, insightful look at Deaf identity, co-written by James Caverly and Andrew Morrill. Tim and Jake are Deaf roommates living in a city apartment—but that’s about all they have in common. While one embraces Deaf culture with pride and resistance, the other seeks to navigate the hearing world with different priorities. As they clash over everyday tasks—like taking out the trash—their disagreement grows into a sharply comic, layered exploration of identity, communication, and what it really means to understand someone else’s experience. The play is co-written by James Caverly (known for Only Murders in the Building) and Andrew Morrill, both of whom bring personal insight and authenticity to the characters and their world. Directed by Nathaniel P. Claridad (Once Upon a Mattress at Tran...

shows.syn: Rachel Anne McAdams (born November 17, 1978) is a Canadian actress. A graduate of York University in 2001 with a BFA in theatre, she is known for her starring roles in comedy and drama films as well as her work in television and theater.

shows.syn: *Chinese Republicans* is a biting new satirical drama about the corporate ladder, a group of in-charge businesswomen, and the young woman who disrupts their perceptions of power and money.

shows.t: The Amazing Sex Life of Rabbits
shows.syn: Money, sex, and madness collide when exes reunite for a dinner party. The Amazing Sex Life of Rabbits follows two couples whose seemingly “friendly” dinner spirals into a volatile clash of love, lust, and class warfare. Bobby and Elise are struggling to keep their marriage—and their bank accounts—afloat when Bobby’s ex-wife, now a best-selling sex therapist, invites them to dinner with her new billionaire husband. What begins as an awkward reunion quickly devolves into a night of shocking revelations and psychological warfare, as the couples use every weapon at their disposal—nostalgia, sexuality, and money—to expose each other’s deepest desires and fears. This darkly comic new play unfolds as a modern-day morality test, examining what happens when ambition and desperation collide over ...
*Blackout Songs* is a new play about a couple who meets in AA and the journey of ups and downs they endure together.

"La Llamada" () is a song recorded by American recording artist Selena for her first live album Live! It was composed by Selena y Los Dinos backup singer Pete Astudillo and Selena's brother and principal record producer A. Quintanilla III and produced by Quintanilla III and Argentine music producer Bebu Silvetti.
*Marcel on the Train* is a play about a young Marcel Marceau, one of the world’s greatest mimes, as he grows up in the shadow of rising Nazism during World War II.
*No Singing in the Navy* is a three-person, one-piano musical that spoofs and questions the Golden Age of Musicals.

Four estranged sisters reunite for a chaotic family ritual where secrets, humor, and grief collide. Jesa is a bold and emotionally charged new play that reimagines the idea of a “perfect family gathering.” When four estranged Korean American sisters return to Orange County to perform their father’s Jesa—a traditional ceremony honoring their ancestors—old resentments resurface, buried secrets are unearthed, and both literal and emotional ghosts demand to be faced. Blending sharp wit, explosive humor, and heartfelt honesty, the play explores the messy bonds of family, the weight of tradition, and the struggle to find connection amid conflict.

shows.t: The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee
shows.syn: *The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee* is a charming and witty musical about adolescent students competing to win a local spelling competition while coping with teenage issues along the way.

Spend a day at Dunder Mifflin at this musical spoof on the hit sitcom. All the most famous lines and moments from the show appear in the stage musical, which features songs like “Goodbye Toby, You're the Worsty” and "That's What She Said."

Figure out whodunnit at the longest-running play in New York history. See Catherine Russell, who has set world records for starring in the show since 1987, as a psychiatrist who's the prime suspect in her husband's mysterious, potentially unsolvable murder.

The hero of the drama, Arbenin, is a wealthy middle-aged man endowed with a rebellious spirit and a strong will. Born into high society, he strives in vain to gain independence and freedom. He lives by the laws of his society, and, in trying to defend his honor while blinded by jealousy and pride, ends up murdering his wife.

A haunting opera exploring trauma, loss, and buried secrets in the aftermath of a school shooting. Innocence, the final opera by Finnish composer Kaija Saariaho, is a powerful exploration of the lasting trauma caused by a school shooting. With a libretto by renowned author Sofi Oksanen, the opera unfolds through a hauntingly beautiful soundscape that blends traditional opera, expressionistic speak-singing, and Scandinavian folk music. Set a decade after the tragedy, the story follows a young couple whose wedding unexpectedly reopens old wounds, revealing long-buried secrets. At its core is a grieving mother, portrayed by mezzo-soprano Joyce DiDonato, and her lost daughter, sung by Finnish ethno-pop artist Vilma Jää. Conducted by Susanna Mälkki in Simon Stone’s strikingly direct producti...

A stage adaptation of Susanna Kaysen’s memoir set inside a 1960s psychiatric hospital. This world-premiere adaptation of Girl, Interrupted, written by Pulitzer Prize–winning playwright Martyna Majok, brings Susanna Kaysen’s memoir to the stage with original music by Aimee Mann and choreography by Sonya Tayeh. The play follows Susanna’s admission to a psychiatric institution, where she navigates an environment that functions as both a safe haven and a source of confinement. As she forms relationships with other young women in the ward, the work examines their efforts to regain structure, autonomy, and a sense of direction within the hospital’s constraints. Directed by Jo Bonney, the production presents a new dramatization of Kaysen’s account.
*Cold War Choir Practice* is an intriguing play about espionage, roller disco, and cults over a family reunion during the holidays.
*11 to Midnight* is a dance-theatre show about ringing in the new year with friends old and new, with promises to keep before time runs out.

shows.t: What We Did Before Our Moth Days
shows.syn: *What We Did Before Our Moth Days* is Wallace Shawn's new play about family and the kaleidoscopic journeys we make through remorse, sorrow, and joy.

is immortalized as Elsinore in the play Hamlet]] ===Act I=== Prince Hamlet of Denmark is the son of the recently deceased King Hamlet, and nephew of King Claudius, his father's brother and successor. Claudius hastily married King Hamlet's widow, Gertrude, Hamlet's mother, and took the throne for himself.

A daring Chekhov-inspired satire directed by Alexander Molochnikov, written by Eli Rarey. SEAGULL: TRUE STORY blends autobiographical drama and sharp political satire with the timeless human questions at the heart of Chekhov’s The Seagull. Written by Eli Rarey and created and directed by Alexander Molochnikov, this inventive retelling dramatizes Molochnikov’s own struggle to stage Chekhov’s masterpiece in the face of artistic and political constraints. What begins as a production of a classic play spirals into a collision of comedy, chaos, and creative defiance. Molochnikov—an internationally acclaimed director from the Moscow Art Theatre—leads an ensemble of international artists in this daring exploration of censorship, identity, and the relentless fight for artistic freedom.
